skill.name: projection-patterns
skill.description: Build read models and projections from event streams. Use when implementing CQRS read sides, building materialized views, or optimizing query performance in event-sourced systems.
skill.summary: Expert guidance on building projections and read models for event-sourced systems using CQRS patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: This skill contains documentation and Python code examples for CQRS projection patterns. Static analyzer flagged markdown code blocks as shell commands (false positive). URLs are documentation references, not network calls. No executable code, cryptographic operations, or system access detected. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Build CQRS Projections and Read Models
content.value_statement: Struggling with query performance in event-sourced systems? This skill provides proven patterns for building efficient read models and projections from event streams.
actual_capabilities: Explain CQRS projection architecture and component roles
Provide Python code templates for building projectors
Guide implementation of checkpoint-based event processing
Show patterns for Elasticsearch and SQL read models
Demonstrate aggregation and multi-table projection techniques
Validate projection idempotency and error handling
limitations: Provides guidance only - does not execute code or connect to databases
Code examples are Python-focused - other languages require adaptation
Assumes familiarity with event sourcing fundamentals
Does not provide production monitoring or observability setup

use_cases.title: E-commerce Order Projections
use_cases.description: Build read models that aggregate order events into customer-facing summaries with real-time status updates.
use_cases.target_user: Backend engineers implementing order management systems

use_cases.title: Search Index from Events
use_cases.description: Create Elasticsearch projections that keep product catalogs in sync with domain events for full-text search.
use_cases.target_user: Developers building search functionality in event-sourced applications

use_cases.title: Analytics Dashboard Data
use_cases.description: Implement aggregating projections that compute daily metrics like sales totals and user activity.
use_cases.target_user: Data engineers building real-time analytics dashboards

prompt_templates.title: Basic Projection Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to build a read model for order events. Show me how to create a basic projector that handles OrderCreated and OrderUpdated events, stores checkpoints, and updates a SQL table.

prompt_templates.title: Elasticsearch Integration
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a projection that syncs product domain events to Elasticsearch. Include handling for ProductCreated, ProductUpdated, and ProductDeleted events with proper error handling.

prompt_templates.title: Aggregation Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me how to build an aggregating projection that computes daily sales metrics from OrderCompleted events. Include UPSERT logic for efficient updates.

prompt_templates.title: Multi-Table Transactional Projection
prompt_templates.prompt: I need a projection that updates multiple tables atomically when processing CustomerOrderPlaced events. Show transaction handling and rollback patterns.
prompt_templates.scenario: Complex domain with related read models

output_examples.input: User asks for a basic projector template
output_examples.output: Provides a Python Projection base class with abstract methods for name, handles(), and apply(). Includes a Projector runner with checkpoint management and batch processing. Shows async/await patterns for database operations.

output_examples.input: User needs an order summary projection
output_examples.output: Delivers OrderSummaryProjection class handling OrderCreated, OrderItemAdded, OrderShipped events. Each handler updates specific columns. Demonstrates parameterized SQL queries and connection pooling.

output_examples.input: User wants to rebuild a corrupted projection
output_examples.output: Explains rebuild process: delete checkpoint, optionally clear read model tables, re-run projection from position 0. Emphasizes idempotency requirement for safe replay.

best_practices: Make projections idempotent - safe to replay events multiple times without side effects
Store checkpoints after each event to enable resume after failures
Use database transactions for multi-table updates to maintain consistency
anti_patterns: Coupling projections together - each projection should be independent and isolatable
Skipping error handling - unhandled exceptions stop projection and cause data lag
Processing events out of order - always respect global_position ordering

faq.question: What is the difference between a projection and a read model?
faq.answer: A projection is the process that transforms events into a read model. The read model is the resulting data structure (database tables, cache, search index) optimized for queries.

faq.question: How do I handle events that arrive out of order?
faq.answer: Use global_position from the event store to ensure strict ordering. Reject or buffer events that arrive before their predecessors. The event store guarantees order, not the message bus.

faq.question: Can I rebuild a projection if the read model gets corrupted?
faq.answer: Yes. Delete the projection checkpoint and clear the read model tables, then re-run from position 0. This requires projections to be idempotent.

faq.question: Should projections be synchronous or asynchronous?
faq.answer: Use asynchronous processing for scalability. Projections typically run as background workers consuming from the event stream, not in the write transaction.

faq.question: How do I handle projection failures?
faq.answer: Log errors with full event context, alert on repeated failures, and implement retry logic with exponential backoff. Consider a dead-letter queue for poison events.

faq.question: What database should I use for read models?
faq.answer: Choose based on query patterns: PostgreSQL for relational queries, Elasticsearch for full-text search, Redis for caching, or column stores for analytics. Multiple projections can use different databases.
